subject: AP Psychology
theme: Sensation is the process of detecting physical energy from the environment, while perception is the brain's interpretation and organization of that sensory information into meaningful experiences.
core_concepts: Sensation begins when sensory receptors transduce physical energy (light, sound, pressure, chemicals) into neural signals that travel to the brain for processing.
Absolute threshold is the minimum stimulation needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time; difference threshold (JND) is the minimum change needed to notice a difference between two stimuli.
Weber's Law states the difference threshold is a constant proportion of the original stimulus — larger stimuli require larger changes to be noticed ($\Delta I / I = k$).
Signal detection theory explains how we distinguish signals from noise, influenced by both sensitivity and response bias (motivation, expectations, fatigue).
Vision depends on rods (dim light, peripheral vision) and cones (color, detail, bright light) in the retina; the optic nerve carries signals to the visual cortex in the occipital lobe.
Hearing involves sound waves vibrating the eardrum, ossicles, and cochlear hair cells, which transduce vibrations into neural signals sent via the auditory nerve to the temporal lobe.
Gestalt principles (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, figure-ground) describe how the brain automatically organizes sensory input into meaningful patterns and objects.
Top-down processing uses prior knowledge and expectations to interpret stimuli, while bottom-up processing builds perception from raw sensory data — both operate simultaneously.

key_terms.definition: The process by which sensory receptors detect and transduce physical energy from the environment into neural signals.
The brain's process of organizing and interpreting sensory information to give it meaning.
The conversion of physical energy (light, sound, pressure) into neural impulses that the brain can interpret.
The minimum amount of stimulation needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time.
The minimum difference between two stimuli required for detection 50% of the time; also called the just noticeable difference.
The principle that the just noticeable difference is a constant proportion of the original stimulus intensity ($\Delta I / I = k$).
A theory predicting how and when we detect faint signals amid background noise, accounting for both sensitivity and decision criteria.
Diminished sensitivity to an unchanging stimulus over time (e.g., no longer noticing background noise).
Retinal receptor cells that detect dim light and provide peripheral and night vision; not sensitive to color.
Retinal receptor cells concentrated in the fovea that detect fine detail and color in bright light.
The theory that color vision depends on three types of cones sensitive to red, green, or blue wavelengths.
The theory that color perception is controlled by opposing pairs (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white) of neural processes.
The fluid-filled, spiral-shaped inner ear structure containing hair cells that transduce sound vibrations into neural signals.
The theory that different frequencies of sound activate hair cells at different locations along the basilar membrane.
The theory that the rate of nerve impulses traveling up the auditory nerve matches the frequency of a sound wave.
The theory that the spinal cord contains a neural 'gate' that can block or allow pain signals to pass to the brain.
Rules describing how the brain organizes sensory input into meaningful patterns: proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, figure-ground.
Information processing guided by higher-level knowledge, expectations, and experience to interpret sensory input.
Information processing that begins with raw sensory data and builds up to the brain's recognition of patterns and meaning.
A mental predisposition to perceive things in a certain way, influenced by expectations, context, and prior experience.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests the distinction between sensation and perception — remember that sensation is detection and transduction of stimuli, while perception is the brain's interpretation. Also know both theories for color vision and hearing, as questions often ask which theory explains specific phenomena.
